summary: Joel Spolsky argues that picking a ship date should be done early (even arbitrarily), then used as a forcing function to cut low-priority features rather than slipping the schedule. He outlines three release cadences — frequent small releases for small teams, 12-18 month cycles for commercial software, and 3-5 year cycles for platforms — and explains the tradeoffs driving each. A key insight is that commercial products should avoid shipping too early because first impressions are nearly permanent, using Marimba Castanet as a cautionary tale. He also warns against doing features in order of fun rather than priority, and notes that even web-delivered software shouldn't change too frequently because it undermines usability and user expectations.
key_points: A ship date should be set early and used as leverage to cut low-priority features, not as a moving target that slips with scope creep
Features must be built in priority order, not in order of programmer fun, or you end up with impressive easter eggs but no core functionality

Small teams with few customers should prefer frequent small releases to get fast feedback, while commercial products with many customers should prefer less frequent releases to avoid weak first impressions
First impressions of software are nearly permanent — shipping an anemic 1.0 can permanently damage a product's reputation even if later versions are excellent
Even web-delivered software shouldn't update too frequently, because constant UI changes violate user expectations and reduce usability
money_quotes: If you do this well, you'll soon discover that you don't regret the features that you cut. They're usually kind of dumb.
It's like editing. If you ever want to write a brilliant 750 word essay, start by writing a 1500 word essay and then edit.

If you aren't careful, your programmers are going to do features in order of fun, and you won't be able to ship or cut features because all the programmers were busy writing Karaoke Recalc before they even got the menus to work.
As a customer, the only thing better than getting feature requests done quickly is getting them instantaneously because they're already in the product, because it was designed thoughtfully.
If you ship an anemic commercial program just to get something out the door so you can 'start listening to customers,' what you'll hear those customers saying is 'it doesn't do very much.'
Five years down the line, people will still remember their first impression of 1.0, and it will be almost impossible to get them to reevaluate.
An application is usable if it behaves the way that the user expected it to behave. If you keep changing things around every week, it won't be so predictable, so it won't be so usable.
key_insight: A fixed ship date is a prioritization tool: set it early, build features in priority order, and cut ruthlessly to ship on time — because the features you cut are almost never missed.
